coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of an organic pollutant by carbon dot/g-C3N4 composites
coredata.dc:creator: Hsu, Je-Wei
Wei, Ling-Wei
Chen, Chiaying
Wang, H. Paul
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants using the solar energy is environmentally and economically attractive for developing an energy sustainable organic wastewater treatment process. Here, we employed the impregnation-thermal method to prepare novel carbon dot (CD) dispersed graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (GCN)/pyromellitic dianhydride (PDI) (CD/GCN/PDI) composites for photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 and on-site oxidation of organic pollutants in contaminated or waste water. After a 5-h visible-light irradiation, 252 μM of H2O2 can be yielded by the CD/GCN/PDI composites. The CD, serving as an electron reservoir, dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., CD0.003/GCN/PDI) facilitates the separation of photo-excited electrons to promote the two-electron reduction O2-to-H2O2 (O2→˙O2 −→H2O2). Most importantly, it also on-site activates H2O2 to form ·OH radicals (H2O2→·OH) to enhance oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A). The ·OH, ∙O2 −, and photogenerated h+ account for 4–5%, 14–18%, and 20–25% oxidation of organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation for 3 h, respectively. The CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite was also tested for photocatalytic oxidation of the organic pollutant under visible-light irradiation for at least 12 h to demonstrate its photostability and reusability. This visible-light photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 for the enhanced oxidation of organic pollutants by the low-cost and metal-free CD/GCN/PDI composites using solar energy was developed to demonstrate the feasibility of an energy self-sufficient organic wastewater treatment process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon dots
Advance oxidation processes
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the CD (a, b) and CD0.003/GCN/PDI composites (c, d).
XRD patterns of the (a) GCN, (b) GCN/NDI, (c) CD0.006/GCN/PDI, (d) CD0.003/GCN/PDI, (e) CD0.0015/GCN/PDI and (f) GCN/PDI photocatalysts.
FTIR spectra of the (a) CD0.006/GCN/PDI, (b) CD0.003/GCN/PDI, (c) CD0.0015/GCN/PDI, (d) GCN/PDI, (e) GCN/NDI and (f) GCN photocatalysts.
UV/Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the (a) GCN/NDI, (b) GCN/PDI, (c) CD0.003/GCN/PDI and (d) GCN photocatalysts.
PL emission spectra of the (a) GCN, (b) GCN/NDI, (c) GCN/PDI, (d) CD0.0015/GCN/PDI, (e) CD0.003/GCN/PDI and (f) CD0.006/GCN/PDI photocatalysts.
(A) electrochemical impedance spectra and (B) Mott-Schottky plots of the (a) GCN, (b) GCN/PDI and (c) CD/GCN/PDI photocatalysts.
Time-dependent photocatalytic generation of H2O2 effected by the (a) CD0.003/GCN/PDI, (b) GCN/PDI, (c) GCN/NDI and (d) GCN photocatalysts.

Time-dependent photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue (3 × 10−5 M) by the GCN, GCN/PDI and GCN/NDI photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation (following the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics). The oxidation of methylene blue by H2O2 (10 mM) without a photocatalyst under UV-light irradiation was as a reference.
Time-dependent photocatalytic oxidation of (A) methylene blue (6 × 10−5 M) and (B) bisphenol A (10 ppm) by the (a) GCN, (b) GCN/NDI, (c) GCN/PDI and (d) CD0.003/GCN/PDI photocatalysts.
Photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue (6 × 10−5 M) effected by the (a) CD0.006/GCN/PDI, (b) GCN/PDI, (c) CD0.0015/GCN/PDI and (d) CD0.003/GCN/PDI photocatalysts.

Time-dependent photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue (1.2 × 10−4 M) by the (a) GCN/PDI and CD0.003/GCN/PDI composites with (b) potassium iodide (KI), (c) p-benzoquinone (BQ), (d) tert-butanol (TB) (2 × 10−4 M) and (e) without a quencher under visible-light irradiation for 180 min.

Time-dependent fluorescence emission spectra under visible light irradiation in a terephthalic acid (5 × 10−3 M) solution effected by the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite (excitation at 315 nm) for (a) 0, (b) 60, (c) 120 and (d) 180 min. The time-dependent fluorescence intensities at 425 nm are shown in the inset.

Photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of an organic pollutant (R) by the CD/GCN/PDI composites. The CD, serving as an electron reservoir, dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., CD0.003/GCN/PDI) facilitates the separation of photo-excited electrons to increase the two-electron reduction O2-to-H2O2 (O2→˙O2 −→H2O2). Most importantly, it also on-site activates H2O2 to form ·OH radicals (H2O2→·OH) to enhance oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A). Other on-site activated highly reactive species such as ∙O2 − and photogenerated h+ also contribute to oxidation of organic pollutants in wastewater.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of an organic pollutant by carbon dot/g-C3N4 composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, 70101, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung, 40227, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung 40227, Taiwan

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Prof. Jiri Jaromir Klemeš

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants using the solar energy is environmentally and economically attractive for developing an energy sustainable organic wastewater treatment process. Here, we employed the impregnation-thermal method to prepare novel carbon dot (CD) dispersed graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (GCN)/pyromellitic dianhydride (PDI) (CD/GCN/PDI) composites for photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 and on-site oxidation of organic pollutants in contaminated or waste water. After a 5-h visible-light irradiation, 252 μM of H2O2 can be yielded by the CD/GCN/PDI composites. The CD, serving as an electron reservoir, dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., CD0.003/GCN/PDI) facilitates the separation of photo-excited electrons to promote the two-electron reduction O2-to-H2O2 (O2→˙O2 −→H2O2). Most importantly, it also on-site activates H2O2 to form ·OH radicals (H2O2→·OH) to enhance oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A). The ·OH, ∙O2 −, and photogenerated h+ account for 4–5%, 14–18%, and 20–25% oxidation of organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation for 3 h, respectively. The CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite was also tested for photocatalytic oxidation of the organic pollutant under visible-light irradiation for at least 12 h to demonstrate its photostability and reusability. This visible-light photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 for the enhanced oxidation of organic pollutants by the low-cost and metal-free CD/GCN/PDI composites using solar energy was developed to demonstrate the feasibility of an energy self-sufficient organic wastewater treatment process.

Photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of an organic pollutant (R) by the CD/GCN/PDI composites. The CD, serving as an electron reservoir, dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., CD0.003/GCN/PDI) facilitates the separation of photo-excited electrons to increase the two-electron reduction O2-to-H2O2 (O2→˙O2 −→H2O2). Most importantly, it also on-site activates H2O2 to form ·OH radicals (H2O2→·OH) to enhance oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A). Other on-site activated highly reactive species such as ∙O2 − and photogenerated h+ also contribute to oxidation of organic pollutants in wastewater.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are feasible for wastewater treatments, especially for oxidation of persistent organic pollutants (Giwa et al., 2021). To have a better effectiveness, AOPs using highly reactive oxidants (e.g., ozone) are frequently integrated with methods such as UV, Fenton and photocatalysis (Bhat and Gogate, 2021). Oxygenated free radicals, for example, ·O2 −, ·O2H and ·OH, attributed to its high reduction potential (2.86 V), are very reactive species for oxidation of organic pollutants (Escobar et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2022). The ·OH radicals, being extremely unstable (a very short lifetime of 10−9 s), can be obtained promptly from H2O2 for a clean oxidation process that emits only O2 and H2O (Ma et al., 2021; Coha et al., 2021).

H2O2 is conventionally manufactured by the Anthraquinone (AO) method involving multi-steps including hydrogenation, oxidation, extraction and regeneration (Anantharaj et al., 2021). Although the AO process may achieve high H2O2 yield rate, the high energy consumption remained as the main challenge. The price of the industrial grade H2O2 was as high as $390–500 per ton (Zhao et al., 2021). Simultaneous generation of H2O2 and on-site utilization for AOPs using the unlimited solar energy makes this method highly applicable and feasible as an energy self-sufficient organic wastewater treatment process (Kou et al., 2022). In the photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 process, photo-excited electron (e−) and hole (h+) pairs are formed by absorbing light energy that is greater than the band-gap energy of a photocatalyst. Subsequently, the h+ can oxidize H2O to O2 that involves in the two-electron reduction step to yield H2O2. The solar-driven H2O-to-H2O2 process requires effective photocatalysts with a well-controlled two-electron reduction step (Yang et al., 2017).

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) (GCN), a metal-free polymeric photocatalyst, has been widely studied for photocatalytic H2 and H2O2 generation, and photocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants (Elsayed et al., 2021; Raizada et al., 2020; Sudhaik et al., 2020; Hasija et al., 2020; Fattahimoghaddam et al., 2021). Also, GCN with a narrow bandgap energy (2.7 eV) can be prepared by the facile and non-toxic method (Hasija et al., 2021). In the photocatalysis process, GCN forms 1,4-endoperoxide intermediates that suppress the one-electron transfer resulting to the formation of H2O2 (Hsu et al., 2022). However, the photocatalytic activity of GCN is still far from the necessity of engineering applications due to the rapid recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Thus, modifying pristine GCN with non-metal doping such as carbon- and nitrogen-enriched species (i.e., pyridinic or graphitic species) can improve photocatalytic effectiveness and carrier separation rate (Cheng et al., 2022; Feng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021).

H2O2 can be generated through a two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) (O2 + 2H+ + 2e− → H2O2, E = +0.68 V SHE) that is thermodynamically feasible. However, H2O2 for oxidation of organic pollutants suffered from relatively low conversions (Shi et al., 2021). Carbon dot (CD) being quasi-spherical graphite nanoparticles (<10 nm) with desired electrons transfer properties can effectively reduce the recombination rate of photoexcited e−-h+ pairs to facilitate photocatalytic reactions (Du et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2022a,b). In particular, CD had unique catalytic characteristics toward activation of H2O2 that can induce the formation of highly reactive species (HRS) such as hydroxyl free radicals (·OH) (Pirsaheb et al., 2018). This inspired the design and preparation of novel photocatalysts for simultaneous photocatalytic generation of H2O2 and on-site utilization for oxidation of organic pollutants. In the present work, CD dispersed GCN composites were prepared by a facile impregnation-thermal method. Aromatic diimides, being a critical classification of n-type organic semiconductors, has good electron affinity which can enhance charge separation (Shiraishi et al., 2021). The photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 reaction could be enhanced by incorporating aromatic diimides (pyromellitic dianhydride (PDI) to GCN. The CD as the catalytic sites for activation of H2O2 were dispersed on the GCN/PDI composites to enhance the oxidation of organic pollutants. Additionally, the reaction kinetics for the photocatalytic oxidation of an organic pollutant by the composites were also investigated for further engineering applications.

The CD was prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid (anhydrous, J.T. Baker) and ethylenediamine (99%, Alfa Aeasr) using the one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, citric acid (4.2 g) (98%, Sigma-Aldrich) and ethylenediamine (1340 μL) were dissolved in deionization water (40 mL). Subsequently, the solutions were sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 453 K for 5 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the yielded brown CD solution was cooled down to 298 K, and further purified by a dialysis bag (500–1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 35 h. The purified CD solutions were dried at 323 K to obtain the CD brown powder.

The GCN powder was prepared by thermal condensation of melamine (99%, Alfa Aesar). Melamine (about 10 g) was heated in a porcelain cup at a heating rate of 2 K/min to 823 K for 4 h. The formed light-yellow solid was ground to fine powder in a mortar to yield the GCN powders. The GCN/PDI and GCN/NDI composites were prepared by thermal copolymerization of melem with aromatic diimides (pyromellitic dianhydride (PDI) (97%, Sigma-Aldrich) or 1,4,5,8-naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (NDI) (97%, Alfa Aesar), respectively. Melamine (about 10 g) was heated in a semi-closed porcelain cup at a heating rate of 7 K/min to 698 K for 4 h to obtain melem. A mixture of the melem and PDI at the melem/PDI weight ratios of 1:2, 1:1 and 2:1 was heated in a semi-closed porcelain cup at a heating rate of 1 K/min to 598 K for 4 h to yield the GCN/PDI composites (i.e., GCN/PDI (1-1), (1–2) and (2–1)). For comparison, mixtures of melem and NDI at the melem/NDI weight ratios of 1:3, 1:1 and 3:1 were prepared in a semi-closed porcelain cup at a heating rate of 1 K/min to 598 K for 4 h to obtain the GCN/NDI composites (i.e., GCN/NDI (1-1), (1–3) and (3–1)). The CD dispersed GCN/PDI composites (CD/GCN/PDI) are prepared by the facile impregnation-thermal method. Melem (1 g) and PDI (2 g) (GCN/PDI (1–2)) were mixed in a semi-closed porcelain cup containing the CD solutions (3 mL) (0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 mg/mL), which was heated at a heating rate of 1 K/min to 598 K for 4 h to obtain the CDn/GCN/PDI composites with n representing the CD weight fractions of 0.0015, 0.003 and 0.006.

The absorbance spectra of the powder photocatalysts were studied on a diffuse reflectance UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 100) with an integrating sphere apparatus using BaSO4 as the background reference. The lamp source with the double-path beam mode ranged from 200 to 800 nm was used. The band-gap energy of the photocatalysts was obtained by converting the absorbance spectra to the Tauc plot. To observe the recombination rate for the photo-excited electrons and holes of the photocatalysts, their photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by micro-Raman spectroscopy (Labram HR, JobinYvon) using an excitation wavelength of 375 nm laser as the excitation source. The electrochemical measurements were analyzed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, 727D). Briefly, the photocatalysts (20 mg, approximately) were stirred with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) (2 mL) containing polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) (2 mg) for 1 h to form a slurry, which was slowly dripped onto a glass carbon electrode (GCE, 0.2 cm2) and dried at 353 K for the photoelectrode. With the electrolyte (Na2SO4 (0.5 M)), reference electrode (Ag/AgCl (KCl (3M))) and counter electrode (Pt), the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the photoelectrodes were measured between 0.001 and 8000 Hz. And, their Mott-Schottky plots were also studied at 1500, 2000 and 3000 Hz.

The crystalline structure of the photocatalysts was studied by X-ray Diffraction spectroscopy (D8 Discover, Bruker AXS Gmbh, Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with the CuKα radiation at a scan rate of 30o/min from 5 to 80o (2θ). The morphologies of the photocatalysts were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (UHRFE-ESM AURIGA) at an accelerating voltage from 0.1 to 30 kV. The samples were coated with a gold film to enhance conductivity for SEM (JEOL, JSM-6700) measurements. The morphologies and particle sizes of the samples were measured by TEM (JEOL JEM-2100F Cs STEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) for the element analysis.

Photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 was studied under visible-light irradiation at 298 K. About 40 mg of the photocatalysts were used in a cylindrical glass reactor containing deionized water (40 mL). Prior to the photocatalytic experiments, deionized water was purged with O2 gas and stirred in dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the reactor which was then irradiated with visible-light (λ > 400 nm) using a 500 W Xe lamp (Burgeon Instrument Co., Ltd). About 1 mL of the suspension was periodically sampled and filtered with a filter of 0.22 μm for measurements of H2O2 concentrations by the copper-DMP spectrophotometric method. Briefly, the concentrations of H2O2 were obtained using the maximum absorbance at 454 nm by reacting with a complex containing copper(II) ion (99%, Sigma-Aldrich) and 2,9-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline (99%, Alfa Aesar) in the copper-DMP procedure (Kosaka et al., 1998; Wei et al., 2022). This method can be effectively used in the wide range pH (5–9) with advantages of insensitivity to other species, high stability, and rapid reactivity. About 10 mg/L of the DMP solution (0.5 mL), 0.01 M of copper(II) sulfate (0.5 mL), phosphate buffer solution (0.5 mL, pH 7), deionized water (1 mL), and suspension withdrawn from the reactor (1 mL) were mixed in a quartz tube having a 1-cm light path. The absorbance at 454 nm was measured on a UV/Vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary 100). A calibration curve from the absorption spectra of H2O2 was used.

Methylene blue (>82%, Sigma-Aldrich) and bisphenol A (BPA) (97%, Sigma-Aldrich) were used to simulate organic pollutants in contaminated or waste water. About 40 mg of the photocatalysts were added in the cylindrical glass reactor containing the organic pollutant solution (40 mL). Prior to the oxidation experiments, the suspension was stirred in dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, which was then irradiated with visible-light (λ > 400 nm) using a 500 W Xe lamp (Burgeon Instrument Co., Ltd) at 298 K. The suspension was sampled at a time interval and centrifuged at 10000 ppm to remove particles from water for the absorbance measurements at 664 nm on a UV/Vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary 100). Concentrations of methylene blue and bisphenol A were determined on a UV/Vis spectrometer (at 664 nm, Varian, Cary 100) and high-performance liquid chromatography with UV detector (at 268 nm, HPLC, Thermo Dionex Ultimate 3000), respectively. Additionally, p-Benzoquinone (BQ) (98%, Alfa Aesar), potassium iodide (KI) (99%, Sigma-Aldrich), and tert-butanol (TB) (99%, Sigma-Aldrich) (2 × 10−4 M) were used as quenchers for superoxide free radical (·O2 −), photo-excited holes (h+) and hydroxyl free radical (·OH), respectively, to study the role of highly reactive species (HRS) in the photocatalytic reactions (Schneider et al., 2020). Also, terephthalic acid (5 × 10−3 M) that was used for the ·OH trapper was studied on a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer LS-55, USA).

Morphologies of the CD prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine were studied by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The HRTEM images in Fig. 1 (a) and (b) show that the as-synthesized CD is monodispersed on the carbon coated grids with an average particle diameter of 5 ± 2 nm. The lattice space of the CD is 0.304 nm related to the 002 diffraction plane of the sp2 graphitic carbon (Shi et al., 2022). In Fig. 1(c), a stacked 2D layered structure for the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., GCN/PDI (1–2)) is observed. The pristine GCN has a relatively smooth surface. As the CD was added to the GCN/PDI, the π-π stacking interactions can be observed in Fig. 1(d) (see the red arrow). In addition, a few large particles dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite may be due to the fact of CD aggregation during the thermal polymerization process.

Crystalline structures of the GCN composites are shown in Fig. 2 . Typically, the GCN has two main peaks at 13.0o and 27.3o which can be assigned to the in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units and regularly graphitic stacking of the aromatic CN units with an interlayer distance of 0.670 and 0.325 nm, respectively (Bellamkonda et al., 2019). The XRD pattern of the GCN/NDI composite (i.e., GCN/NDI (1-1)) is similar to the GCN, suggesting that they possess a basically analogous crystalline structure. With the combination of the PDI, the intensity of the peak at 27.3o decreases. Also, two new peaks at 18.8o (d = 0.467 nm) and 29.4o (d = 0.302 nm) are observed corresponding to the π-π stacking of the PDI units and interactions between melem and PDI units, respectively (Shiraishi et al., 2021). In addition, the distance of interlayer for the stacking of the aromatic CN units is slightly changed after the CD dispersion. The peak at 27.3o shifts to the higher 2θ, especially for the CD0.0015/GCN/PDI and CD0.003/GCN/PDI composites, which can be attributed to the interactions of CD during the thermal copolymerization of PDI and melem.

The FTIR spectra of the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . The absorbance features at 1772, 1725 and 726 cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric stretching, symmetric stretching and bending vibrations of imide carbonyl groups (CO), respectively (Zhou et al., 2019). The absorbance feature at 1376 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of C–N–C in the five-membered imide rings. The typical stretching modes of the CN heterocycles (CN) are observed at 1635 and 1574 cm−1 (Su et al., 2016). The absorbance of the feature at 810 cm−1 is related to the characteristic mode of the triazine units. A broad feature centered at about 3200 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration modes of the secondary and primary amines (-NH) (Sekizkardes et al., 2019).

The UV/Vis absorption spectra of the photocatalysts that have absorbance in the visible-light region are shown in Fig. 4 . With the incorporation of aromatic diimides units, the absorption edge of the GCN has an apparent red-shift for an effectively harvesting visible-light. The band-gap energies obtained by the absorption spectra are 2.57 and 2.42 eV for the GCN and GCN/PDI composite, respectively.

The PL spectra of the photocatalysts in Fig. 5 were measured under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The emission wavelength of all samples was in the range of 350–700 nm. The PL intensity of the pristine GCN decreases as aromatic diimides units (PDI and NDI) were incorporated. In Fig. S1, the N1 peaks at 398.5, 400.3, and 404.3 eV are associated with pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic species, respectively (Zhang et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2022a,b). The GCN/PDI composites have more pyridinic and graphitic active sites for ORR than the GCN/NDI composites. Also, the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite has a lower PL intensity than GCN/PDI, indicating that the CD, being an electron reservoir, can reduce the recombination rate of photoexcited e−-h+ pairs.

The EIS of the GCN, GCN/PDI, and CD/GCN/PDI photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 6 (A). Compared to the GCN and GCN/PDI, the CD/GCN/PDI composite has a relatively small semicircle radius with better separation of photoexcited e− and h+. In Fig. 6(B), the Mott-Schottky plots of the photocatalysts show positive slops, serving as the n-type semiconductors. The CD/GCN/PDI composite has a flat band (Efb) of −0.71 V vs. NHE suggesting a relatively small kinetic overpotential (EO2/-O2- = 0.38 V) to enhance photocatalytic H2O2 generation.

The reactor, containing the photocatalysts (40 mg) in deionized water (40 mL), was irradiated by visible-light under the O2-saturated condition at 298 K. Concentrations of photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 were monitored by the copper-DMP method. In Fig. 7 , the GCN/PDI composite increases H2O2 generation linearly under visible-light irradiation. The better photocatalytic performance of the GCN/PDI composites may be attributed to the high electron affinity for charge separation and desired band-gap energy for the high selectivity of two-electron reduction of O2 (Shiraishi et al., 2021). The photocatalytic H2O2 generation was enhanced (accumulated 252 μM of H2O2 within a 5-h visible-light irradiation) by the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite due to the rapid charge transfer with the CD. For example, with the addition of CD to GCN/PDI (e.g., CD0.003/GCN/PDI), the photocatalytic H2O2 yields are significantly increased by approximately 80% (at 60 min), 45% (at 120 min), 29% (at 180 min), 25% (at 240 min), and 15% (at 300 min) under visible-light irradiation. The H2O2 yield, however, has a non-linear growth trend that is associated with the decomposition of H2O2 by CD on the composite. Additionally, the CD possessed a unique catalytic behavior for activating H2O2 to HRS such as ·OH− (Liu et al., 2018, 2021; Pirsaheb et al., 2018).

Methylene blue, being a common industrial dye material and difficult to be removed by traditional wastewater treatment processes, was used as the representative organic pollutant for this work in photocatalytic oxidation under visible-light irradiation. Fig. 8 shows that the photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue follows the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics. The GCN-based composites have much greater reaction rates for photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue under visible-light irradiation than the reference (H2O2 (10 mM) without a photocatalyst under UV-light irradiation). Particularly, the GCN/PDI composites show higher photocatalytic oxidation rates than the GCN/NDI composites. It is notable that the GCN/PDI composite has a higher photocatalytic H2O2 yield (see Fig. 7) and thus a higher oxidation rate than the GCN/PDI composite.

Although the H2O2 yielded from photocatalysis is a thermodynamically feasible oxidant for oxidation of methylene blue, the reaction kinetic rate for oxidation of organic pollutants by H2O2 was, however, relatively slow. This composite provides on-site utilization of H2O2 to facilitate oxidation of the organic pollutants. The time-dependent photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue and bisphenol A (10 ppm) (a colourless pollutant) effected by the CD dispersed GCN/PDI is shown in Fig. 9 . Methylene blue and bisphenol A with a similar conversion performance can be effectively oxidized by the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite under visible-light irradiation. The rapid oxidation of methylene blue by the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite may be associated with the high yield H2O2 shown in Fig. 8, and H2O2 can be activated by CD to yield the HRS ·OH for enhancing oxidation.

Fig. 10 shows the photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue by the CD/GCN/PDI composites. For the CD0.0015/and CD0.003/GCN/PDI composites, methylene blue was photocatalytically oxidized under visible-light irradiation for 3 h. However, the CD0.006/GCN/PDI composite, which contains more CD that may increase the recombination rate of photoexcited e−-h+ pairs (Fig. 5), has a relatively low photocatalytic oxidation conversion compared to the GCN/PDI composite. Similar observations of excessive CD loading leading to low photocatalytic oxidation were reported (Fang et al., 2016). Moreover, the photostability and reusability of the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite for photocatalytic oxidation of an organic pollutant (e.g., bisphenol A) were characterized by a four-cycle run as shown in Fig. S2. A similar reaction trend of the continuous four-run photocatalytic oxidation effected by the composite is observed signifying its high photostability and reusability for at least 12 h.

Effect of HRS such as hydroxyl free radical (·OH) photo-excited holes (h+) and superoxide free radical (·O2 −) on the photocatalytic oxidation were studied by the quencher experiments. The p-benzoquinone (BQ), potassium iodide (KI), and tert-butanol (TB) (2 × 10−4 M) were added to the photocatalytic cell for the quench of ·O2 −, h+, and ·OH, respectively. In Fig. 11 , the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite can indeed boost the photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue (6 × 10−5 M). With the addition of TB, the photocatalytic oxidation of methylene blue was slightly suppressed (4.9% approximately) by the quench of ∙OH. However, in the presence of BQ, the oxidation efficiency of MB decreased to 12.3%, indicating that the ∙O2 − was a key species in the photocatalytic oxidation reaction. Besides, the observation for the lower (16.6%) photocatalytic oxidation conversion with KI (a scavenger for the photogenerated h+) suggests that h+ also played an important role in the photocatalytic reactions. The GCN/PDI composite has the conduction and valance band positions of −1.12 and 1.67 V, respectively. As the H2O/∙OH potential (E = 2.8 V vs. SHE) is more negative than the conduction band of the GCN/PDI, the ·OH− radicals may be generated from H2O2 (H2O2/∙OH E = 0.38 V vs. SHE). In Fig. 12 , terephthalic acid, being a chemical probe to form a highly fluorescent product, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, was also used to capture the ∙OH− radicals under visible-light irradiation by the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite. An increasing intensity of fluorescence at 425 nm with the irradiation time ratifies that the CD0.003/GCN/PDI composite was feasible for H2O2 activation to yield ∙OH.

The photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of an organic pollutant by the CD/GCN/PDI composites is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The VB and CB energy levels of the GCN/PDI composite are −1.12 and 1.67 V vs. SHE, respectively for harvesting visible-light to generate photo-excited charges. Note that the potentials of O2/∙O2 − (−0.33 V vs. SHE), H2O2/∙OH (0.38 V vs. SHE), and O2/H2O2 (0.68 V vs. SHE) are within the bandgap energy levels of the GCN/PDI composite indicating the occurrence of such reactions. The CD, serving as an electron reservoir, dispersed on the GCN/PDI composite (i.e., CD0.003/GCN/PDI) facilitates the separation of photo-excited electrons to increase the two-electron reduction O2-to-H2O2 (O2→˙O2 −→H2O2). Most importantly, it also on-site activates H2O2 to form ·OH radicals (H2O2→·OH) to enhance oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A). The ·OH, ∙O2 −, and photogenerated h+ account for 4–5%, 14–18%, and 20–25% oxidation of organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation for 3 h, respectively.

The CD dispersed GCN/PDI composites were prepared and used for photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 synchronized oxidation of organic pollutants. The low-cost and metal-free CD/GCN/PDI composites enhance photocatalytic H2O2 generation via the two-electron O2 reduction process. About 252 μM of H2O2 can be yielded under visible-light irradiation for 5 h. Carbon dots, serving as an electron reservoir, can effectively retard the recombination rate of photo-excited charges and facilitate activation of the highly reactive species ·OH (4–5%) and ∙O2 − (14–18%) as well as photogenerated h+ (20–25%) that contribute to oxidation of organic pollutants (e.g., methylene blue and bisphenol A) under visible-light irradiation for 3 h. The continuous four-run photocatalytic oxidation effected by the composite was observed signifying its high photostability and reusability for at least 12 h. The visible-light photocatalytic H2O-to-H2O2 system for on-site oxidation of organic pollutants using solar energy was developed to further enhance the accessibility for an energy-sufficient organic wastewater treatment process.

Je-Wei Hsu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ling-Wei Wei: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Chiaying Chen: Visualization, Supervision. H. Paul Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:H. Paul Wang reports financial support was provided by Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology.
